Design Systems · AI · Benevity

Scaling Skyline for the Age of AI

How I transformed Benevity's design system from a developer bottleneck into a self-sufficient platform — then built the visual language and component system that brought AI into production.

40+
Components Documented
2
AI Features Shipped
15+
Designers Unblocked
1
Unified AI Visual Language
Company
Benevity
Role
Senior Product Designer
Team
Skyline Design System
Timeline
2022 – Present
AI Work
2024 – Present
Context

A system with no front door

I joined Benevity as Skyline 2 was launching — a design system supporting 15+ product designers across a 40–60 component library. But the infrastructure around it told a different story. Documentation lived on a custom-coded website that only developers could update. Content went stale within weeks. Designers had nowhere to self-serve answers.

The result: multiple interruptions every single day — pings to me and the two other Skyline developers asking which component to use, where specs lived, whether the docs were still current. We were the system. That wasn't sustainable.

"The documentation required a developer to update it. Which meant it never got updated. Which meant no one trusted it."
The problem — old documentation site

The existing site required a developer to make any update — meaning it was almost never updated, and designers couldn't trust what they found there.

⚠ Before — Old doc site
Developer-only doc site Fully custom-coded. Any update required a developer. Stale within weeks of any component change.
⚠ Before — Old doc site
No reliable source of truth Designers couldn't tell what was current, deprecated, or in progress. The system lost their trust entirely.
The challenges I inherited
🔒

Developer-only updates

Only the 3 Skyline developers could edit documentation — a permanent bottleneck for a 15+ person design team.

🔄

Stale by default

Because updates were costly, they rarely happened. Designers couldn't trust what they found — docs were months behind.

📣

Constant interruptions

Multiple pings per day. Questions that should have been self-servable were consuming hours of design systems bandwidth.

🤖

AI features incoming

Leadership was investing in AI features with no visual language established. Someone needed to build the foundation from scratch.

Phase 1 — Self-Service

Building a system designers could actually use

Rather than relying on a developer-gated website, I built a multi-layer ecosystem that put documentation control back in the hands of the wider team. The goal: any designer should be able to answer their own question — without pinging anyone, without waiting for a developer.

Google Docs Write-ups Human-readable docs any team member can edit. No more staleness, no more developer gatekeeping.
Figma Visual Examples Component references showing real product contexts — End User vs Admin, with page references to live screens.
Storybook Integration Live component playground with variants, states, and API docs. Real behaviour — not static specs.
Figma UI Kit 50 components marked Ready for Dev, with Visual Examples showing components in real product contexts.
SkyDocs — AI Design System Consultant A NotebookLM AI trained on 70+ Skyline documentation sources. Designers ask natural language questions and get sourced, accurate answers instantly — no developer, no waiting, no guessing. The most innovative layer of the self-service ecosystem.
Phase 2 — AI Components

A new kind of design problem

With the self-service layer in place, leadership assigned a new challenge: Benevity was shipping AI-powered features, and Skyline had no visual language for them. No components, no patterns, no guidance on how AI should look or feel inside the product.

The question wasn't just aesthetic. AI introduces a fundamentally different relationship between system and user — one built on trust, transparency, and managed uncertainty. How do you make AI feel legible without making it feel alarming?

"Don't style components based on what they control. Style them based on what they are."
Research

I audited how leading AI products handle the same problem — Perplexity, Adobe Firefly, Canva — documenting interaction patterns, visual states, and the moments where AI presence needed to be communicated vs. where it would add noise.

Competitive research Auditing Perplexity, Adobe Firefly, and Canva to understand how leading AI products handle prompt patterns, blank-canvas states, and AI presence signalling.
The Framework

Identity vs. Utility

Most products fail at this by over-branding AI or under-communicating it. I built a decision tree to solve this systematically. The key principle: style components based on what they are, not what they control.

Identity vs. Utility decision framework Five component roles, each with a distinct visual treatment and meaning communicated to the user.
Three visual states

Each state communicates a different relationship between the component and the AI system.

Visionary Violet · Identity
"I am AI-native logic."
Solid, flat. Used for AI badges and trust indicators. Signals AI is present — not currently doing anything.
Gradient Dashed · Gateway
"Drop here for AI analysis."
Dashed multi-colour border. Used where uploading triggers immediate AI analysis. Signals intent, not action yet.
Gradient Animated · Processing
"I am actively thinking."
Solid animated border + shimmer. Any component can enter this state. Signals live work, manages uncertainty.
Component Strategy

Build what matters, when it matters

Rather than building an AI component library speculatively, I established a prioritization system tied directly to the product roadmap. Every component is mapped to a real feature, a user goal, a priority level, and a build status.

Strategy flows into component design → Skyline system integration → global scalability and client brand implementation.

AI component tracker 20+ components mapped across priority, size, feature alignment, content requirements, and build status — tied to EMR, GiveOpps CC, Grants Summary, and AI Search Experience.
Shipped · External Match Request

Receipt scanning & validation

Employees donating to nonprofits outside Benevity's platform were manually entering donation details to request an employer match — friction-heavy and error-prone. AI now reads the uploaded receipt and auto-populates the form.

This feature became the proof of concept for the entire AI visual framework. Every pattern established here is now the template for all future AI features across the product.

State 01
Ready to receive
AI badge establishes trust before any action. Dashed gradient border signals this upload triggers AI intelligence.
State 02
File received
Drag confirmation appears. The gradient border activates — the system has accepted the file and is ready to process.
State 03
AI is working
Animated border + shimmer bar. "Checking your receipt for name, amount, date, and nonprofit…" — transparent and specific.
Shipped · Grants Summary

AI grant application scoring

Grant reviewers were reading through lengthy nonprofit proposals manually — scoring, summarising, and making funding decisions under time pressure. AI now generates a scored evaluation for each application across four criteria, with full reasoning shown.

The design challenge was trust. A number alone means nothing. Every score needed to show its work — cited evidence, criteria descriptions, a full summary. The Inline Rationale pattern is the key: every claim is traceable.

Components at work

AI Score + Badge

84/100 with a Beta label. Visionary Violet signals AI authorship. The score is a starting point — not a verdict.

Dimensional Breakdown

Four criteria scored independently. Progress bars give instant visual hierarchy — reviewers see strengths and gaps at a glance.

Inline Rationale + Cited Evidence

Every score includes the AI's reasoning and direct quotes from the proposal. The system shows its work.

AI Summary

Structured overview: applicant, funding request, objectives, expected impact, challenges. Complete context without opening the proposal.

Architectural Decisions

Built to last — and to let go

Two decisions defined how the AI system would hold up over time — not just at launch, but as AI becomes table stakes and the product continues to evolve.

🎨

Client branding harmony

Benevity's platform is white-labeled — CN, Amazon, Kohl's, and others bring their own brand palettes. I stress-tested Visionary Violet against high-contrast client themes across 5+ brands.

🔧

Graceful degradation

Every AI component is designed so that stripping the Visionary Violet or animated gradation leaves a fully functional standard Skyline component. Zero engineering rework if AI branding evolves.

My Impact

What this work moved

15+
Designers self-serving without pinging the Skyline team
40+
Components documented across the self-service ecosystem
70+
Sources powering the SkyDocs AI consultant
2
AI features shipped in production using the new component system
4+
Product areas now using the AI framework
1
Unified visual language for AI across Benevity's entire product suite
The patterns established in EMR receipt scanning are now the template for GiveOpps Content Creation, Grants Summary, and the AI Search Experience. The work compounds.

This wasn't just a design systems project — it was an infrastructure investment. By solving the self-service problem first, I freed the Skyline team to do higher-leverage work. By establishing the AI visual framework before products shipped, I gave Benevity's product teams a foundation instead of a series of one-off decisions made under deadline pressure.
